Relive the Moment · ALCS Game 4 · Oct 17, 2004

The Steal

Dave Roberts had one job. Everyone in America knew it. He did it anyway.

↓ Scroll to relive it

Screen-grab · MLB video

Three outs from the end of everything.

Roberts takes his lead.

Back. Dive. Again.

He goes.

Safe. By a fingertip.

Hand on the bag.

Mueller lines one up the middle.

Tie game. The season exhales.

On first

Bottom of the ninth at Fenway. The Red Sox trail by a run — and trail the series three games to none. No team in baseball history has ever come back from there. Mariano Rivera is on to end it.

Kevin Millar has worked a walk. Dave Roberts comes in to run for him — one job, and everyone in the ballpark knows exactly what it is. So does Rivera, who throws over to hold him close.

Pickoff after pickoff. Roberts dives back to the bag, dusts himself off, and takes his lead again. The whole park holds its breath on every pitch.

On the next pitch he breaks for second — the most anticipated stolen base of the decade.

The throw beats him. His hand reaches the bag a heartbeat ahead of the tag. Safe. The season is still alive.

Ninety feet from a tie now, in scoring position. The dugout is on its feet. Bill Mueller steps in against Rivera.

A clean single into center. Here comes Roberts, around third, digging for home…

He scores. 4–4. What looked like the end is suddenly a fresh start — and Ortiz is waiting in the twelfth.

Scored — 4–4

He scores. 4–4. What looked like the end is suddenly a fresh start — and Ortiz is waiting in the twelfth.

COMP · Getty / NY Daily News Archive

Three innings later, Ortiz ended it. The greatest comeback in baseball history had its first heartbeat.

Names & Numbers
Game: ALCS Game 4 · Boston vs. New York · Oct 17, 2004
The play: SB (2nd), bottom 9th — pinch-runner Roberts
Scored on: Bill Mueller RBI single — tie game, 4–4
Ending: Ortiz walk-off 2-run HR (12th) — Sox win 6–4
Rights status (tracker): sharp frames are Getty comps (Pensinger / Jacobsohn); softer ones are MLB-video grabs. All Gray · Do not use yet. The credit tag on the image changes per frame.
